focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Carrow was peeling an orange when they came in. He stood behind his desk with his sleeves pushed up past the forearms, digging his thumbnails into the rind and dropping strips of peel onto the wood in damp curls. The classroom smelled of citrus and, underneath it, the flat mineral odour that never left the stones anymore — scorched air, old copper, the residue of whatever had happened in here during the last lesson. The fifth-years filed in and sat along the walls.

The desks had been shoved against the stone since November, clearing the center of the room into an open floor that Gavin had stopped thinking of as a teaching space and started thinking of as a place where things happened to people. Students sat on the flagstones with their backs to the wall, schoolbags between their knees, wands out because you needed your wand out for Dark Arts or Carrow noticed. Gavin took his usual spot near the back left corner, where a torch bracket cast enough shadow that Carrow's gaze tended to skip past him. It wasn't reliable. Nothing was reliable. But the shadow felt like something, and this year Gavin took what he could get.

Duncan was already seated three places to his left. He'd been reading something when Gavin came in — not a textbook, something smaller, a folded piece of paper he'd been holding close to his face in the way of someone with a private thing in a public place. He put it away when the room started filling, tucked it into his trouser pocket with a quick, practised motion. It was a letter from home, probably. Duncan's parents wrote through the Muggle post, which got screened at the gates, and the letters that made it through were rare enough that Duncan carried them in his pocket for days. Gavin had seen him refolding one once, creasing the paper al...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...had not been pressing hard enough to leave them. When he bent his fingers they moved a fraction late, stiff and white.

Somewhere in the castle behind him his friends were sleeping — Miren with the hand warmer on her bedside table, probably cool by now because the charm had a half-life of about two hours, and Duncan in the purple scarf because the dormitory was cold and the scarf was warm, and Malcolm Preece with his blankets pulled to his eyebrows and his one foot sticking out. The fudge tin was on Gavin's trunk with three pieces gone. The book about the demolished planet lay face-down on the arm of the chair.

The wards were thinner than they had been.

He didn't know how he knew it in any way he could prove. The manuscript had described degradation in notation he could barely follow — terms that had no English equivalents, structural relationships he had to hold in his head in four dimensions and watch collapse back to three — but the knowledge was in his body now. The window frame had a little give under his hand, not enough to shift, just enough to tell him where the strain was, and he could feel that give in the wards as well. They were still holding, just less completely than before. The margin between what held and what pressed had narrowed. Not by much. Not enough to see or measure or prove to anyone.

He stood at the window long enough for the numbness in his hand to creep into his wrist. Frost kept branching from the lead strips in thin white lines, and somewhere down by the greenhouses the ice ticked again, small and brittle in the dark. Behind him the castle slept in its own warmth, held inside something that had less to spare than it had yesterday.

He pulled the curtain across the window and went back to bed, but the feel of that give stayed in his hand.
